story:
Winter had passed, but it was never the cold that kept John Parker awake. The carved rock beneath him steadied his mind as he watched the Peruvian Andes glow white with distant calm. Only five of them had made it this high. Only five, after the world fell.

By the time scientists learned that altitude was the only shield, it was already too late. It began in Mali. Was it really unforeseeable? The splendor of old kings and the swagger of modern elites, all swept away, their wealth and power no defense against the collapse they had helped shape. It ended in a fortnight. As if the animal kingdom had cast a vote and found humanity unfit. The pathogen spared everything that wasn’t us. Only Homo sapiens fell.

It started like a cold. A mild cough. A fever no one feared. The monster hid in plain sight, dormant until it brushed new, healthy cells. Grey lips. Eyes pushing out like they wanted to escape. That was when it was already too late. Then came the madness. The laughter. The kind that made strong people run. “Laughing Cannibals”—the last headline John remembered.

Scans came later, when there were still scanners. The brain it left behind wasn’t human. Twisted folds, wrong angles. The children fell fastest. The pathogen hunted them, preferring young cells to old. People thought the sick were choosing easy prey. They were wrong. The virus took three days to seize the controls. Then it walked and smiled and spoke through its host like a puppeteer with perfect strings. No one suspected a thing, not until the hunger announced itself. And when it did, there was no shame, only delight.

Carrie Parker was twelve. Short velvet hair, always tidy. “Yes, Daddy,” she’d say, willing and bright, her olive eyes shining like morning dew. The day John’s car slid into their drive, the front door hung open. Bloody fingerprints curled down the hall like red surf. In the kitchen, Carrie was feeding. The tiles were wet with his wife’s blood. Meat between his daughter’s small teeth. Her eyes still Carrie’s, as if she were herself and not. She had been a good girl, he told himself. He kept saying it, as if the words could anchor him to the mountain instead of pulling him over the edge. A single step and it would all be quiet. Would it be a kindness to stop?

Five remained. Beth Cooper, all freckles and gentle hands, kinder than anyone he’d met. Caitlyn McLeod, a bright-eyed art student who should have lived in studios and galleries instead of caves and snow. Bill and Roger Bank, brothers who survived a mine collapse that swallowed others by the thousands along the high plain. Grief and rage clung to them like storm clouds. They knew things John didn’t want to ask about.

There would never be a cure. He’d seen it in Carrie’s gaze—the knowledge that something else had taken the wheel. Those who couldn’t run were gone or going. The only refuge left was up. Their flight lay broken at the foot of the glacier, the last of their luck spent to bring them here. Years had passed. The nights still stretched.

John rose and followed the ice path toward camp. Laughter carried to him, thin and strange. Was his mind finally cracking? No. They were laughing. When he reached the fire, he saw it—real cheer, faces lit from within. Teeth worn down, smiles still stubborn. It looked, impossibly, like a beginning. Like the species turning a page. For the first time in three years, John Parker smiled.
